{"step_by_step_thinking": "The relevant information is found in Document [0], which states that the corneal epithelium contains thousands of nerve endings that are innervated by the long ciliary nerves from the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve (V1). The long ciliary nerves are part of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve, which innervates the cornea. Among the options provided, the infraorbital nerve (A), supraorbital nerve (B), and infratrochlear nerve (C) are all branches of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve, but they do not specifically mention innervating the cornea. The nasolacrimal nerve (D) is likely a printing mistake and probably refers to the nasociliary nerve, which is indeed a branch of the ophthalmic division of the trigeminal nerve and specifically innervates the cornea.", "answer_choice": "D"}
